column1: What does C have instead of modules?
column2: Translation units (.c files compiled separately) glued by the linker, with .h files as the hand-maintained contracts between them.

A declaration introduces a name and its type (promises it exists somewhere) and may appear many times. A definition actually creates it (storage for a variable, body of a function) and may appear exactly once — the One-Definition Rule.

column1: What is the one-sentence rule for headers vs source files?
column2: Headers carry declarations, source files carry definitions.
An include guard, type definitions (typedef/struct/union/enum), macros and static inline functions, function prototypes for public functions, and extern declarations of public globals.

column1: What must a header NOT contain?
column2: Function bodies or (non-inline) variable definitions — a header included by N source files would then create N definitions and the linker rejects the duplicates.

column1: What is an include guard and why?
column2: #ifndef NAME / #define NAME / ... / #endif. A header may be reached more than once (A includes B and C, both include D), and the guard makes the second inclusion a no-op so types are not redefined. (#pragma once does the same in one line.)

column1: What belongs in a source file (.c)?
column2: It includes its own header first (so the compiler checks definitions against declarations), defines the public functions and the one real definition of each public global, and holds private helpers/state marked static (internal linkage).

External (default for file-scope functions/globals) makes a name visible to the linker and shareable across files. Internal (static at file scope) makes it private to its translation unit — two files can each have a static int count with no clash.

column1: What are the two unrelated meanings of static?
column2: At file scope it means internal linkage (private to the file). On a local variable it means static storage duration (persists across calls, lives in .data/.bss, not the stack). Same keyword, two meanings — context tells you which.

column1: How do you correctly share a global across files?
column2: Put a declaration extern int g in the header, and the single definition int g = 0 in exactly one .c file. Putting int g = 0 in the header instead defines it in every including file — a multiple-definition link error.

column1: What is a function prototype and why is it mandatory?
column2: A declaration that includes the parameter types. Without it, a pre-C99 compiler implicitly assumes int return and accepts any unchecked arguments, so a wrong call (e.g. passing a double where a pointer is expected) becomes a run-time catastrophe instead of a compile error.

column1: How does a missing prototype reinterpret your bits?
column2: Calling double scale(double) as int y = scale(2) with no prototype passes the integer 2 where a double is expected and reads an int result — the bit patterns are reinterpreted as garbage, no warning, crashing far away. Including the header makes the compiler convert and check.
